text: hence the notion of Homo Ludens: ‘Play is older than culture, for culture, however inadequately defined, always presupposes human society, and animals have not waited for man to teach them their playing’, as Huizinga put it. The spirit of playful competition is, as a social impulse, older than culture itself and pervades all life like a veritable ferment. Ritual grew up in sacred play; poetry was born in play and nourished on play; music and dancing were pure play ... . We have to conclude, therefore, that civilization is, in its earliest phases, played. It does not come from play ... it arises in and as play, and never leaves it. (Huizinga 1949: 21) It was exactly this dimension that Gregory Bateson incorporated in his epistemology, expressed in his famous essay On a Theory of Play and Fantasy (2000[1955]), among others. Drawing on his empirical research on animal communication, Bateson concluded that play (and even the use of metaphor) lies at the origin of both animal and human evolution, grounding our communication and learning processes. In all its seriousness, play is of course also fun; but again, this ‘fun’ 6 Voegelin reviewed Homo Ludens in Journal of Politics in 1948; it remained an important reference for Voegelin throughout his career, not least in the Order and History series. Voegelin praises Homo Ludens as ‘one of rare great works that advances substantially our understanding of man in historical and political existence’. Liminality and the Modern 150 cannot be broken down into categories and analytically dissected. It simply is; the fun is part of that ‘flow’ experience which Victor Turner later described. Huizinga identified five characteristics that play must have: 1. Play is free – is in fact freedom. 2. Play is not ‘ordinary’ or ‘real’ life. 3. Play is distinct from ‘ordinary’ life both as to locality and duration. 4. Play creates order, is order. Play demands order, absolute and supreme. 5. Play is connected with no material interest, and no profit can be gained from it. This is a meaningful characterization, both because it coheres with Plato’s analysis, and because it serves to stress the liminal nature of the playful as distinct from the ordinary in spatial, temporal and experiential terms. Play is a stepping- out of ‘real’ life into a temporary sphere of activity with a disposition all of its own. Importantly, Huizinga is expressly not saying that ‘everything is play’; the entire phenomenon is only possible because of distinction and the upholding of a boundary. As Huizinga puts it: ‘real civilization cannot exist in the absence of a certain play-element, for civilization presupposes limitation and mastery of the self, the ability not to confuse its own tendencies with the ultimate and highest goal, but to understand that it is enclosed within certain bounds freely accepted’ (1949: 211). The five points also read like this: no freedom, no play; no distinction between ordinary and extra-ordinary, no play; no spaces or times for play in distinction to normality, no play; no order, no play; if there is material interest or profit-making attached to the activity, then it is not play. Already here, of course, the problems pile up with respect to contemporary culture. Huizinga operates several other distinctions concerning play, and one is particularly important for our purposes here, namely that between competitive games and games of pure chance: In games of pure chance the tension felt by the player is only feebly communicated to the onlooker. In themselves, gambling games are very curious subjects for cultural research, but for the development of culture as such we must call them unproductive. They are sterile, adding nothing to life or mind. (Huizinga 1949: 48) This ‘unproductive’ nature of games of pure chance 7 should not let us underestimate their significance. In fact, in the first appendix to Man, Play and Games, Caillois 7 We use the term ‘pure chance’ to distinguish games like lottery and roulette from various forms of betting on events (such as horse races or football matches) where a degree of participation and involvement with the game often accompanies the gambling; these are still games of chance – and the participant is ‘passive’ – but not ‘pure’ chance. Game and Gambling and the Implosion of Liminality: Playing Modernity 151 discusses precisely The Importance of Games of Chance, seen from a comparative cultural perspective. Caillois notes how it is just such games of chance that under certain circumstances, ‘assumes sufficient importance to partly determine the life- style of an entire society’ (Caillois 2001: 145). One basic aspect of Bateson’s general communication theory must be stressed here, namely the role of frames. Play is defined by a frame around an activity, which changes the meaning of acts and words within that frame. Animals and humans do that in many ways, and most often the framing process is extra-linguistic. Animals know when they are playing for fun: cats avoid using their claws, for example. As Bateson showed, animals are very efficient in communicating to each other that ‘this is play’, and so are children. Adults are probably a bit slower on that point. Children sometimes verbalize the frame before they enter the game, for example: ‘Let us say that you were the mother and I was the baby’. I have noted how children almost always signal their frame by switching from present to past tense in most languages, and often also by altering the pitch of their voice just slightly. At certain moments of the play, they will turn to what Bateson calls meta-communication, as they address (with normal pitch and in present tense) some of the rules of the play, before they ‘enter’ the frame again: once in it, they are in it. It is very boring to play with people who don’t understand this; and children are very aware when adults lose play-concentration. The frame is a message about the message, and children know this by instinct. It is universal and no doubt perennial. Still with reference to the
